title: Quiz: ADMET Properties
description: Test your understanding of ADMET prediction in drug discovery

questions.question: According to Lipinski's Rule of Five, what is the maximum molecular weight for good oral bioavailability?
questions.explanation: Lipinski's Rule of Five states that orally bioavailable drugs typically have MW ≤ 500 Da, LogP ≤ 5, H-bond donors ≤ 5, and H-bond acceptors ≤ 10. These rules were derived from analysis of marketed oral drugs.

questions.question: Which CYP enzyme is responsible for metabolizing approximately 50% of marketed drugs?
questions.explanation: CYP3A4 is the most important drug-metabolizing enzyme, responsible for approximately 50% of drug metabolism. It has a large binding site and can accommodate many drug-like molecules.

questions.question: What does P-glycoprotein (P-gp) do?
questions.explanation: P-glycoprotein is an efflux transporter that pumps drugs OUT of cells. It's found at the blood-brain barrier, intestine, and other barriers. P-gp substrates have reduced CNS exposure and potentially reduced oral absorption.

questions.question: Why is hERG channel inhibition a critical safety endpoint?
questions.explanation: hERG channel inhibition can cause QT prolongation and potentially fatal cardiac arrhythmias (Torsades de Pointes). This is a major reason for drug withdrawals from the market, making it a critical early screen.

questions.question: A compound with one Lipinski Rule violation is automatically unsuitable as a drug candidate.
questions.explanation: False. The Rule of Five allows for 0-1 violations while still predicting good oral bioavailability. Many successful drugs have one violation. The rules are guidelines, not absolute requirements.

questions.question: Which properties favor blood-brain barrier penetration? (Select all)
questions.explanation: BBB penetration favors small (MW < 400), non-polar (PSA < 90 Ų) molecules that are not P-gp substrates. LogP should be 1-3 (moderate lipophilicity); very high LogP (>5) actually reduces BBB penetration due to membrane retention.
